! About this 3D view

This is an approximate 3D schematic — not a true reconstruction
A faithful 3D model needs the original cath‑lab DICOM (the same vessel filmed from ≥2 known C‑arm angles, then triangulated). Those angles are not in the PDF. Here, the front‑view coronary tree is lifted into 3D using textbook coronary anatomy — LAD & diagonals sit forward, the circumflex & marginals wrap left‑and‑back, the RCA wraps right then back to the PDA. Vessel depth is therefore typical anatomy, not this patient's exact geometry. For the most grounded view, see the 2D map →

★ Independent evidence‑based view

CABG is the guideline‑supported choice — but it's a Heart‑Team call
For this pattern — complex multivessel disease (RCA chronic total occlusion + 80% circumflex + tight diagonal/marginal branches), preserved heart function, non‑diabetic — the major guidelines and trials line up with the report's CABG recommendation, mainly because surgery achieves more complete revascularisation across diffuse and totally‑occluded vessels. PCI is a reasonable alternative if a Heart‑Team / SYNTAX assessment judges the anatomy PCI‑amenable or surgical risk is high. Either way, optimal medical therapy + correcting the thyroid first is essential.
CABG (bypass) Preferred · Class I
Favoured for complex multivessel disease with a CTO and diffuse distal disease — grafts bypass the whole diseased segment and the blocked RCA territory at once (Class I).1,2 SYNTAX/SYNTAXES support surgery as anatomic complexity rises.5
Multivessel PCI Alternative
Reasonable if the Heart Team deems the anatomy suitable / SYNTAX low–intermediate or surgical risk is high. CTO‑PCI of the RCA relieves symptoms but shows no survival benefit (DECISION‑CTO, EURO‑CTO).7,8 FFR guides borderline lesions; here most are ≥80%.4
Optimal medical therapy Foundation
ISCHEMIA (2020): in stable CAD with preserved EF, revascularisation did not cut death/MI vs meds, but did relieve angina.3 With a strongly‑positive stress test, revascularisation (CABG) is justified on top of OMT. FREEDOM (CABG>PCI) is diabetics‑only.6

Bottom line. The report's CABG call aligns with current evidence for this complex, totally‑occluded, multivessel anatomy. Confirm via Heart‑Team + SYNTAX, correct hypothyroidism, optimise meds first. Not medical advice.

The blockages — tap to focus

100% (CTO) Sub‑total / tight ~80% Mild

Reported normal / mild

LMCA — Normal LAD prox & distal — Normal LAD mid — Mild D3 & D4 — Normal LPDA — Mild

What the report says — plainly

A dye study through the wrist artery filmed the heart's supply pipes. The muscle pumps normally with no failure — but several arteries are narrowed or blocked.

The right coronary artery (RCA) is 100% blocked at its start (chronic total occlusion); that area now survives on tiny natural detour vessels from the left side — so the left arteries work overtime, and they are also diseased.

On the left: the circumflex (LCX) is 80% narrowed near its origin with diffuse disease beyond; its big marginal (OM1) is 80%; two diagonal branches are critically tight (D1 nearly blocked, D2 tight). The front artery (LAD) is largely spared — only mild mid disease.
